First Trainer 2 — Practice Test 2Speaking
⏱ ~14 min total ← Menu
Part 1 — Interview  ·  2 minutes (3 minutes for groups of three)  ·  One student plays the examiner and reads the interlocutor questions. The other student answers. Then swap roles.
Note: the timer below resets for each candidate so you can practise both sets of questions in full — in the real exam, the 2 minutes covers both candidates together.
Set A

Candidate A answers

2 min
Interlocutor
First, we'd like to know something about you.
  • Do you have a favourite colour for clothes? (Why?)
  • What do you wear when you relax?
  • Do you enjoy spending time on your own? (Why? / Why not?)
  • Tell us about a day you've really enjoyed recently.
Candidate A speaks
~30 seconds per question
2:00
Tip: Give full answers with reasons and examples. Don't just say yes or no. Don't prepare answers in advance.
⇄ Swap roles
Set B

Candidate B answers

2 min
Interlocutor
First, we'd like to know something about you.
  • Do you have favourite clothes you like to wear? (When do you wear them?)
  • What do you wear when you go to parties? (Why?)
  • What do you enjoy doing with other people?
  • Tell us about a place you really enjoyed visiting. (What did you do there?)
Candidate B speaks
~30 seconds per question
2:00
Part 2 — Long Turn  ·  4 minutes (6 minutes for groups of three)  ·  Each candidate speaks about their two photos for 1 minute. Then the other candidate answers a short question (~30 sec).
Candidate A — Photos C8

People using laptops

1 min + 30 sec
Interlocutor says to Candidate A
Here are your photographs. They show people using laptops. I'd like you to compare the photographs, and say why you think the people are using the laptops. All right?
Why do you think the people are using the laptops?
Candidate A speaks (1 min)
Compare both photos, address the question
1:00
Interlocutor asks Candidate B (~30 sec)
Do you often use a laptop?
Candidate B responds (~30 sec)
0:30
Tip: Try to keep talking for the full minute. If you can't find the right word, paraphrase.
⇄ Now Candidate B takes their turn
Candidate B — Photos C9

People eating together

1 min + 30 sec
Interlocutor says to Candidate B
Here are your photographs. They show people eating together. I'd like you to compare the photographs, and say what you think the people are enjoying about eating together. All right?
What do you think the people are enjoying about eating together?
Candidate B speaks (1 min)
1:00
Interlocutor asks Candidate A (~30 sec)
Do you enjoy eating meals with other people?
Candidate A responds (~30 sec)
0:30
Part 3 — Collaborative Task  ·  4 minutes (5 minutes for groups of three)  ·  Talk together about the question, then decide on the most important factor.
Part 3

Discussion and decision

4 min
Interlocutor
Now, I'd like you to talk about something together for about two minutes. Here are some things that people think about when deciding whether it's better to become an expert in one subject or to know a lot about many things, and a question for you to discuss.
Discuss together — 2 min
Is it better to become an expert in one subject or to know a lot about many things?
Both candidates discuss (2 min)
Try to cover all five points
2:00
Interlocutor — Decision stage (1 min)
Now you have about a minute to decide which two things are the most important when considering this question.
Both candidates decide (1 min)
Disagreement is fine
1:00
Tip: Encourage your partner — Do you agree? What do you think? Don't worry if you haven't covered all five points when time is up.
Part 4 — Further Discussion  ·  4 minutes (6 minutes for groups of three)  ·  Take turns answering. Use the prompts to invite your partner to join in.
Part 4

Broader questions on the topic

4 min
  • Is there a subject that you'd like to be an expert in? (Why? / Why not?)
  • What personal qualities are needed to become an expert in something?
  • Is there someone you particularly admire who's an expert in something?
  • What are the advantages of doing an online course?
  • What do you think about the reliability of online information when you are researching a subject? (Why? / Why not?)
  • Why do you think people do general knowledge quizzes?
Prompts
  • What do you think?
  • Do you agree?
  • And you?
Both candidates (4 min)
Develop each other's answers
4:00
Tip: Give yourself thinking time — That's an interesting question… Let me think…

Tick everything you achieved. Be honest.
